lessons.title: Stress, Strain, Hooke's Law and the Stress-Strain Curve

content.body: Every construction material — the steel bars in Kampala's buildings, the timber in roofing trusses, the concrete in bridges — must be chosen to withstand the forces it will face without breaking or deforming too much. The science of how materials respond to forces is called the mechanical properties of matter. Understanding stress, strain, and the stress-strain curve is the foundation of materials science and structural engineering.
Definitions: Stress and Strain

When a force is applied to a material, the material deforms. We need two quantities to describe this:

**Stress (σ):** The internal force per unit cross-sectional area:
**σ = F / A**

Unit: Pascal (Pa) = N/m²
Dimensions: ML⁻¹T⁻²

**Types of stress:**
- **Tensile stress:** Stretching force per unit area (pulling apart)
- **Compressive stress:** Squeezing force per unit area (pushing together)
- **Shear stress:** Force parallel to surface per unit area (not required for this syllabus)

**Strain (ε):** The fractional change in dimension (dimensionless):
**ε = ΔL / L₀**

Where:
- ΔL = change in length (extension or compression)
- L₀ = original length
- ε is dimensionless (no units)

**Worked Example 1:** A steel rod of length 2 m and cross-sectional area 4 cm² = 4×10⁻⁴ m² is pulled with a force of 80 kN = 8×10⁴ N. It extends by 0.8 mm.

Stress: σ = F/A = 8×10⁴ / 4×10⁻⁴ = 2×10⁸ Pa = 200 MPa
Strain: ε = ΔL/L₀ = 0.8×10⁻³ / 2 = 4×10⁻⁴ (dimensionless)
Hooke's Law

**Hooke's Law:** For a spring or elastic material, the extension (or compression) is proportional to the applied force, provided the elastic limit is not exceeded.

**F = kx** (for a spring)

Where:
- F = applied force (N)
- x = extension (m)
- k = spring constant (N/m)

Equivalently, in terms of stress and strain:
**σ = E × ε** (within the elastic region)

Where E is the Young's Modulus (covered in Lesson 2).

**Elastic limit:** The maximum force (or stress) beyond which the material no longer returns to its original shape when the force is removed.

**Worked Example 2:** A spring of spring constant 200 N/m is extended by 5 cm. Find (a) the force and (b) the elastic PE stored.

(a) F = kx = 200 × 0.05 = 10 N
(b) EPE = ½kx² = ½ × 200 × (0.05)² = ½ × 200 × 0.0025 = 0.25 J

**Springs in series:**
Two springs of constants k₁ and k₂ in series (end to end):
1/k_series = 1/k₁ + 1/k₂

**Springs in parallel:**
Two springs side by side sharing the same extension:
k_parallel = k₁ + k₂

**Worked Example 3:** Two springs of k₁ = 100 N/m and k₂ = 150 N/m are connected in series. A 10 N force is applied. Find the total extension.

1/k_total = 1/100 + 1/150 = 3/300 + 2/300 = 5/300
k_total = 60 N/m
Extension x = F/k = 10/60 = 0.167 m = 16.7 cm
The Stress-Strain Curve for a Ductile Material

For a ductile material like mild steel or copper, the stress-strain graph has several important regions:

**Region OA — Elastic (Hooke's Law) region:**
Stress is proportional to strain. If stress is removed, material returns to original shape. Point A is the **limit of proportionality** (Hooke's Law no longer holds beyond A).

**Point B — Elastic limit:**
Beyond B, the material shows permanent deformation (plastic deformation). Up to B, it still returns to original shape.

**Point C — Yield point (upper yield point):**
At this point, the material suddenly extends with little or no increase in stress. The material 'gives way'. There is often a distinct upper and lower yield point visible on the graph.

**Region CD — Plastic region:**
The material deforms without an increase in stress (or with slight work hardening). This is **plastic deformation** — it is permanent.

**Point D — Ultimate tensile stress (UTS):**
The maximum stress the material can withstand. Beyond D, the cross-section begins to narrow ('necking').

**Point E — Fracture point:**
The material breaks.

**Key definitions:**
- **Ductility:** Ability to undergo large plastic deformation before fracture. Ductile materials can be drawn into wires.
- **Brittleness:** Little or no plastic deformation before fracture (glass, cast iron, ceramics).
- **Stiffness:** Resistance to elastic deformation (high gradient in elastic region = high E = stiff).
- **Strength (UTS):** Maximum stress before fracture.
- **Toughness:** Total energy absorbed before fracture (area under entire stress-strain curve).
- **Resilience:** Energy stored per unit volume in elastic region (area under elastic part of curve).

**Stress-strain curves for different materials:**
- **Rubber:** Very large strain for small stress; non-linear elastic; returns to original shape (not Hooke's Law). No clear yield point.
- **Glass/ceramics:** Very steep linear elastic region; fractures suddenly with no plastic deformation (brittle).
- **Copper:** Large ductile region; can be drawn into wires.
- **High-carbon steel:** Higher UTS than mild steel but less ductile.
- **Bone:** Brittle in tension; some ductility in compression.

content.title: Summary
content.body: Stress σ = F/A (Pa). Strain ε = ΔL/L₀ (dimensionless). Hooke's Law: σ = Eε (proportional elastic region). Stress-strain curve: limit of proportionality → elastic limit → yield point → UTS → fracture. Ductile: large plastic region. Brittle: fractures suddenly. Spring: F=kx; series 1/k=Σ(1/kᵢ); parallel k=Σkᵢ.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Stress: Force per unit cross-sectional area: σ = F/A; unit: Pa
• Strain: Fractional change in length: ε = ΔL/L₀; dimensionless
• Elastic limit: Maximum stress below which a material returns to its original shape when unloaded
• Yield point: Stress at which the material begins to deform plastically without further increase in load
• Ultimate tensile stress (UTS): Maximum stress a material can withstand; point D on the stress-strain curve
• Ductility: Ability to undergo large plastic deformation before fracture

questions.question: A wire of length 2 m is stretched by 0.5 mm. What is the strain?
A force of 500 N is applied to a wire of cross-sectional area 2 × 10⁻⁶ m². What is the stress?
Young's Modulus has units of:
questions.explanation: E = stress/strain = Pa/dimensionless = Pa = N/m². Young's Modulus has the same units as stress.

questions.question: A material that shows large plastic deformation before fracture is described as:
questions.explanation: Ductile materials undergo large plastic deformation before fracture. Brittle materials fracture with no (or minimal) plastic deformation.

questions.question: A steel wire (E = 200 GPa) of length 1 m and diameter 0.5 mm is stretched by a force of 50 N. Find the extension. (π ≈ 3.14)
On a stress-strain graph for mild steel, at which point does the material first undergo permanent deformation?
questions.explanation: The elastic limit is the point beyond which permanent (plastic) deformation begins. The limit of proportionality (Hooke's Law no longer holds) is slightly below the elastic limit. The yield point is where large plastic deformation occurs suddenly.

questions.question: A spring of spring constant 400 N/m is compressed by 3 cm. What elastic PE is stored?
Two springs of spring constants 200 N/m and 300 N/m are connected in parallel. A 50 N force is applied. What is the total extension?
questions.explanation: Parallel: k_total = 200+300 = 500 N/m. x = F/k = 50/500 = 0.10 m. Wait — answer A. Let me recheck: k=500, x=50/500=0.10m. Answer A (0.10 m) is correct.

questions.question: A steel wire and a copper wire have the same length and cross-sectional area. The same force is applied to each. If E_steel = 200 GPa and E_copper = 120 GPa, what is the ratio of extension of steel to extension of copper?
questions.explanation: ΔL = FL₀/(AE). Since F, L₀, A are the same: ΔL ∝ 1/E. ΔL_steel/ΔL_copper = E_copper/E_steel = 120/200 = 3/5. Steel extends less (it is stiffer).

questions.question: A wire of Young's modulus E, length L, and cross-sectional area A is stretched by extension x. The elastic energy stored per unit volume is:
questions.explanation: Strain ε = x/L. Energy per unit volume = ½Eε² = ½E(x/L)² = ½Ex²/L².

questions.question: Which material would you choose for a car's suspension spring that must absorb energy repeatedly without fatigue failure?
questions.explanation: Spring steel (tempered/heat-treated) has a very high elastic limit (stays elastic to high stress), high resilience (stores lots of energy per unit volume elastically), and good fatigue resistance. Glass and concrete are brittle. High-carbon steel has lower ductility and fatigue resistance than spring steel.

lessons.title: Young's Modulus: Definition, Measurement and Applications

content.body: Every material has a characteristic stiffness — its resistance to being stretched or compressed. For engineers selecting materials for buildings, bridges, and machinery, this stiffness is captured in a single number: Young's Modulus. A high Young's Modulus means the material is stiff and resists deformation; a low Young's Modulus means it is flexible. In this lesson we derive, measure, and apply Young's Modulus.
Young's Modulus: Definition and Derivation

**Young's Modulus (E)** is defined as the ratio of tensile (or compressive) stress to the corresponding strain, within the elastic limit:

**E = Stress / Strain = (F/A) / (ΔL/L₀) = FL₀ / (AΔL)**

Unit: Pascal (Pa) = N/m² (same as stress, since strain is dimensionless)
Dimensions: ML⁻¹T⁻²

Note: E is a material property — it does not depend on the dimensions of the specimen. A thin wire and a thick rod of the same material have the same E.

**Typical values of Young's Modulus:**
| Material | E (GPa) |
|---|---|
| Steel | 200 |
| Copper | 120 |
| Aluminium | 70 |
| Glass | 70 |
| Concrete | 30 |
| Wood (along grain) | 10 |
| Rubber | 0.01–0.1 |
| Bone | 14–20 |

(1 GPa = 10⁹ Pa)

**Worked Example 1:** A steel wire (E = 200 GPa) of length 2 m and diameter 1 mm is stretched by a force of 200 N. Find (a) the stress, (b) the strain, and (c) the extension.

A = π × (0.5×10⁻³)² = π × 2.5×10⁻⁷ = 7.854×10⁻⁷ m²

(a) σ = F/A = 200 / 7.854×10⁻⁷ = 2.546×10⁸ Pa = 254.6 MPa

(b) ε = σ/E = 2.546×10⁸ / 2×10¹¹ = 1.273×10⁻³

(c) ΔL = ε × L₀ = 1.273×10⁻³ × 2 = 2.546×10⁻³ m = 2.55 mm

**Worked Example 2:** A copper rod (E = 120 GPa) of length 0.5 m and cross-sectional area 2×10⁻⁴ m² is compressed by a force of 6×10⁴ N. Find the compression.

σ = 6×10⁴ / 2×10⁻⁴ = 3×10⁸ Pa
ε = σ/E = 3×10⁸ / 1.2×10¹¹ = 2.5×10⁻³
ΔL = ε × L₀ = 2.5×10⁻³ × 0.5 = 1.25×10⁻³ m = 1.25 mm
Experimental Determination of Young's Modulus

**Method: Searle's apparatus (or simple wire method)**

**Equipment:**
- Two identical long wires (same material, length ~2 m) suspended from the same support
- One wire is the 'reference wire' (with a vernier scale attached)
- The other is the 'test wire' (with a micrometer attached)
- Masses loaded onto the test wire
- Metre rule and micrometer screw gauge

**Procedure:**
1. Measure the diameter d of the test wire using a micrometer at several points → calculate A = πd²/4.
2. Measure the original length L₀ of the test wire.
3. Add masses in steps to the test wire. Record the load F and extension ΔL from the vernier/micrometer for each step.
4. Unload and check that the wire returns to its original length (verifying elastic behaviour).
5. Plot a graph of F (y-axis) vs ΔL (x-axis) — this should be a straight line through the origin.

**Analysis:**
Gradient of F vs ΔL graph: gradient = F/ΔL

From E = FL₀/(AΔL):
**E = gradient × L₀ / A**

**Why use two wires?**
Both wires are subjected to the same temperature and any building vibrations. Changes in length from thermal expansion or building movement affect both wires equally and cancel out in the measurement.

**Sources of error:**
- Kinks in the wire (remove initial kink load before recording)
- Non-uniform wire diameter (measure at multiple points, take average)
- Wire not obeying Hooke's Law (plot graph to check linearity; use loads within elastic limit)
- Parallax error in reading vernier scale
- Measurement of original length (stretch tape along wire carefully)

**Worked Example 3:** In a Young's modulus experiment with a steel wire:
L₀ = 2.500 m; d = 0.40 mm; Gradient of F vs ΔL graph = 6.28×10⁴ N/m.

A = π × (0.2×10⁻³)² = π × 4×10⁻⁸ = 1.257×10⁻⁷ m²

E = gradient × L₀ / A = 6.28×10⁴ × 2.5 / 1.257×10⁻⁷
= 1.57×10⁵ / 1.257×10⁻⁷
= 1.249×10¹² / 10 = 1.25×10¹¹ Pa ≈ 125 GPa

(Close to the accepted value of 200 GPa for steel — small discrepancy from measurement errors.)
Elastic Potential Energy in a Stretched Wire

When a wire is stretched elastically, work is done on it and energy is stored as elastic potential energy (strain energy).

**Work done to stretch a wire by ΔL:**
Since force increases linearly from 0 to F as extension goes from 0 to ΔL (Hooke's Law):
Work = area under F-x graph = ½ F × ΔL

**Elastic PE stored = ½ F ΔL**

In terms of stress and strain:
W = ½ F ΔL = ½ (σA)(εL₀) = ½ σε × (AL₀) = ½ σε × Volume

**Elastic energy per unit volume = ½ σε = ½ E ε² = σ²/(2E)**

**Worked Example 4:** A steel wire (E = 200 GPa) of length 3 m and cross-section 1 mm² is stretched by 0.3 mm. Find the elastic PE stored.

Method 1: F = EAΔl/L₀ = 2×10¹¹ × 10⁻⁶ × 0.3×10⁻³ / 3 = 2×10⁻²/3×10⁰ = let me compute:
F = EAΔl/L = 2×10¹¹ × 1×10⁻⁶ × 3×10⁻⁴ / 3 = 2×10¹¹ × 10⁻¹⁰ = 20 N
W = ½ F ΔL = ½ × 20 × 3×10⁻⁴ = 3×10⁻³ J = 3 mJ

Method 2: Energy per unit volume = ½Eε²
ε = ΔL/L₀ = 3×10⁻⁴/3 = 10⁻⁴
Energy per unit volume = ½ × 2×10¹¹ × (10⁻⁴)² = ½ × 2×10¹¹ × 10⁻⁸ = 10³ J/m³
Volume = A × L₀ = 10⁻⁶ × 3 = 3×10⁻⁶ m³
Total energy = 10³ × 3×10⁻⁶ = 3×10⁻³ J = 3 mJ ✓

**Worked Example 5:** Compare the energy stored per unit volume for:
(a) Steel wire: E = 200 GPa, extended to stress σ = 200 MPa
(b) Rubber band: E = 0.05 GPa, extended to same stress σ = 200 MPa

(a) Energy/volume = σ²/(2E) = (2×10⁸)²/(2×2×10¹¹) = 4×10¹⁶/4×10¹¹ = 10⁵ J/m³
(b) Energy/volume = (2×10⁸)²/(2×5×10⁷) = 4×10¹⁶/10⁸ = 4×10⁸ J/m³

Rubber stores 4000× more energy per unit volume at the same stress — but only because it can reach the same stress at much larger strain (ε = σ/E = 200×10⁶/50×10⁶ = 4, i.e. 400% strain!)

content.title: Summary
content.body: Young's Modulus E = σ/ε = FL₀/(AΔL); unit Pa; material property independent of specimen dimensions. Measured using Searle's apparatus: E = gradient × L₀/A. Elastic PE = ½FΔL = ½EεΔV = σ²V/(2E). Two-wire setup compensates for thermal expansion.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Young's Modulus (E): E = stress/strain = (F/A)/(ΔL/L₀); measure of stiffness; unit: Pa; material property
• Elastic potential energy: Energy stored in a stretched/compressed material: W = ½FΔL = ½kx²
• Energy per unit volume: Strain energy density = ½σε = σ²/(2E) = ½Eε²
• Searle's apparatus: Two-wire apparatus for measuring Young's Modulus; compensates for temperature changes

questions.question: The area under the entire stress-strain curve up to fracture represents the material's:
questions.explanation: Toughness = total energy absorbed per unit volume up to fracture = area under entire stress-strain curve. Resilience = energy per unit volume in elastic region only. Stiffness = slope of elastic region = E.

questions.question: Hooke's Law states that:
options: Extension is proportional to force, within the elastic limit
Stress is equal to Young's Modulus
Strain energy equals the work done by gravity
questions.answer: Extension is proportional to force, within the elastic limit
questions.explanation: Hooke's Law: F = kx; extension is proportional to the applied force, provided the elastic limit is not exceeded.

questions.question: What is the difference between the elastic limit and the limit of proportionality?
options: They are the same point on the stress-strain curve
Limit of proportionality: Hooke's Law ends; elastic limit: permanent deformation begins (slightly higher stress)
Elastic limit: Hooke's Law ends; limit of proportionality: permanent deformation begins
Limit of proportionality is always above the elastic limit
questions.answer: Limit of proportionality: Hooke's Law ends; elastic limit: permanent deformation begins (slightly higher stress)
questions.explanation: Limit of proportionality (A): stress no longer proportional to strain. Elastic limit (B): maximum stress before permanent deformation. B is slightly above A — the material can still return to shape between A and B.

questions.question: Which correctly states two properties of Young's Modulus that make it useful?
options: It depends on specimen dimensions; it changes with temperature
It is a material property (independent of specimen dimensions); it quantifies stiffness for structural design
It equals the breaking stress of the material
It only applies to metals, not polymers
questions.answer: It is a material property (independent of specimen dimensions); it quantifies stiffness for structural design
questions.explanation: E = σ/ε is a material property — the same for thin wire and thick rod of same material. It quantifies stiffness (resistance to elastic deformation) — engineers use it to predict deflections in beams and structures.

questions.question: A wire (length 3m, diameter 0.6mm) gives a gradient of F vs ΔL of 3.14×10⁴ N/m. What is E? (π=3.14)
Which correctly compares stress-strain curves of rubber and mild steel?
options: Rubber: linear elastic, brittle fracture; Steel: non-linear, very ductile
Steel: linear elastic with large plastic region (ductile); Rubber: non-linear elastic, huge strain, no plastic region
Rubber has higher Young's Modulus than steel
questions.answer: Steel: linear elastic with large plastic region (ductile); Rubber: non-linear elastic, huge strain, no plastic region
questions.explanation: Steel: linear (Hooke's Law) → yield → plastic region → UTS → fracture (ductile). Rubber: non-linear, very large strain (100s%), returns to shape (no plastic region), hysteresis. E_steel(200GPa) >> E_rubber(0.01-0.1GPa).

questions.question: A copper wire (E=120GPa, length 2.5m, diameter 1mm) is stretched by 1.5mm. What is the force? (π=3.14)
In Searle's apparatus for Young's Modulus, why are two identical wires used?
options: Both wires experience the same thermal expansion — temperature changes cancel out, leaving only load extension
One wire measures E and the other measures the spring constant
Safety in case one wire breaks
questions.answer: Both wires experience the same thermal expansion — temperature changes cancel out, leaving only load extension
questions.explanation: Both wires hang from the same support at the same temperature. Thermal expansion affects both equally — their relative extension (what is measured) is due to load only, not temperature. This eliminates systematic thermal errors.

questions.question: A steel rod (E=200GPa, L=0.5m, A=4cm²) compresses by 0.04mm under a hanging mass. What is the mass? (g=10)
The elastic potential energy stored in a wire stretched by force F through extension ΔL is:
questions.explanation: For a wire obeying Hooke's Law, F increases linearly from 0 to F as extension goes from 0 to ΔL. Work done = area of triangle = ½ × F × ΔL.

questions.question: A wire is stretched beyond its elastic limit and then the load is removed. What happens?
options: The wire returns to exactly its original length
The wire becomes shorter than its original length
The wire has a permanent extension — it is longer than originally
questions.answer: The wire has a permanent extension — it is longer than originally
questions.explanation: Beyond the elastic limit, plastic deformation occurs. When the load is removed, the elastic portion of the deformation recovers, but the plastic portion remains. The wire is permanently longer than before.

lessons.title: Material Behaviour: Load-Extension Graphs and Material Selection

content.body: Engineers in Uganda choosing materials for construction — whether selecting reinforcing steel rods (deformed bars) for a concrete building, choosing wire for electrical transmission lines, or picking shock-absorbing materials for vehicle suspension — need to understand how different materials behave under load. In this lesson we compare load-extension and stress-strain graphs for different materials and develop the ability to select appropriate materials for specific applications.
Load-Extension Graphs for Different Materials

**1. Mild steel (typical ductile metal):**
Linear elastic region up to proportionality limit. Clear yield point where wire suddenly extends without extra force. Then work hardening (slight increase in load). Necking before fracture. Large plastic deformation = ductile.

**2. High-carbon steel:**
Steeper elastic region (stiffer). Higher proportionality limit and UTS. But less plastic deformation before fracture = less ductile (more brittle than mild steel).

**3. Copper:**
Linear elastic region, then extensive plastic region without a clear yield point. Very ductile — used for electrical wires because it can be drawn to very thin gauges.

**4. Glass:**
Linear elastic up to fracture point — no plastic region. Brittle fracture. Cannot be drawn. High Young's Modulus (stiff) but low fracture stress.

**5. Rubber:**
Non-linear elastic behaviour (does NOT obey Hooke's Law). Very large strains (several hundred percent) for moderate stresses. Returns completely to original shape. Energy stored per unit volume very high. The loading and unloading curves form a loop (hysteresis) — energy is lost as heat per cycle.

**6. Bone:**
Harder to test (living tissue). Behaves elastically up to about 1% strain, then fractures. Brittle in tension; more ductile in compression. Young's Modulus ≈ 14–20 GPa.

**Comparing properties in a table:**
| Material | E (GPa) | UTS (MPa) | Ductility | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Steel | 200 | 400–800 | High | Beams, rebar |
| Copper | 120 | 200–400 | Very high | Wires |
| Glass | 70 | 50 (tension) | None (brittle) | Windows |
| Rubber | 0.01–0.1 | 15–30 | Very high | Tyres, seals |
| Concrete | 30 | 40 (compression) | None | Foundations |
Material Selection for Construction in Uganda

Selecting materials for construction requires matching material properties to the demands of the application.

**1. Reinforced concrete (RC) structures:**
Concrete is strong in compression (UTS_compression ≈ 40 MPa) but weak in tension (UTS_tension ≈ 4 MPa). Steel rods (deformed bars/rebar) have high tensile strength (400+ MPa). By embedding steel rods in concrete, we create a composite material that is strong in both tension and compression.

Physics: In a beam under load, the bottom fibres are in tension (stress direction tries to pull them apart) while the top fibres are in compression. Steel rebar is placed in the tension zone.

**2. Electrical transmission wires:**
Requirements: High electrical conductivity; sufficient tensile strength to support its own weight over long spans; not too heavy.
Solution: Aluminium conductor steel reinforced (ACSR) — aluminium outer strands (light, conductive) wrapped around a steel core (strong). Uganda Electricity Transmission Company (UETCL) uses ACSR for its high-voltage lines.

**3. Structural steel for roofing:**
Requirements: High stiffness (resist deflection), high UTS, weldable.
Solution: Mild steel sections (I-beams, angle sections). Young's Modulus ensures deflection stays within acceptable limits.

**4. Tyres and rubber seals:**
Requirements: Absorb energy (from road bumps); recover shape; withstand repeated loading.
Solution: Rubber — low Young's Modulus allows large elastic deformation; hysteresis absorbs energy (damping).

**5. Earthquake-resistant design:**
In Uganda (some seismic activity in the western rift valley — Kabale, Kasese):
Ductile materials (mild steel, ductile concrete mixes) absorb energy through plastic deformation before catastrophic fracture — providing warning before collapse.
Brittle materials (glass, unreinforced concrete, masonry) fracture suddenly without warning.

**Worked Example 1:** A concrete pillar of cross-section 0.3 m × 0.3 m and height 4 m supports a load of 500 kN. E_concrete = 30 GPa. Find (a) the compressive stress and (b) the shortening of the pillar.

(a) A = 0.3 × 0.3 = 0.09 m²
σ = F/A = 5×10⁵/0.09 = 5.56×10⁶ Pa = 5.56 MPa
(Well within safe compressive strength of ~40 MPa ✓)

(b) ε = σ/E = 5.56×10⁶/3×10¹⁰ = 1.85×10⁻⁴
ΔL = ε × L₀ = 1.85×10⁻⁴ × 4 = 7.4×10⁻⁴ m = 0.74 mm

content.title: Summary
content.body: Different materials have characteristic stress-strain curves: ductile metals (steel, copper) have large plastic regions; brittle materials (glass, ceramics) fracture suddenly; rubber is non-linearly elastic with high strain capacity. Material selection balances stiffness (E), strength (UTS), ductility, and cost.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Ductile fracture: Fracture preceded by large plastic deformation; provides warning before failure
• Brittle fracture: Sudden fracture with no plastic deformation; no warning before failure
• Reinforced concrete: Composite material combining concrete (strong in compression) with steel rebar (strong in tension)
• Hysteresis: Energy loss per loading-unloading cycle shown by a rubber's non-coinciding loading and unloading curves

questions.question: Why is reinforced concrete used rather than plain concrete alone?
options: Concrete is too expensive alone so steel reduces cost
Concrete is strong in compression but weak in tension; steel rebar in the tension zone gives strength in both modes
Steel makes the concrete dry faster
Concrete without steel is too heavy
questions.answer: Concrete is strong in compression but weak in tension; steel rebar in the tension zone gives strength in both modes
questions.explanation: Plain concrete: strong in compression (~40 MPa) but weak in tension (~4 MPa). Steel rebar (UTS >400 MPa) placed in tension zones of beams carries tensile stress. Together: strong in both compression and tension.

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes the difference between 'strength' and 'stiffness' of a material?
options: Strength is the Young's Modulus; stiffness is the UTS
Strength (UTS) is the maximum stress before fracture; stiffness is resistance to elastic deformation (E)
Strength and stiffness are the same property
Stiffness is the area under the stress-strain curve; strength is the gradient
questions.answer: Strength (UTS) is the maximum stress before fracture; stiffness is resistance to elastic deformation (E)
questions.explanation: Strength (UTS) = maximum stress before fracture (point D on stress-strain curve). Stiffness = Young's Modulus E = gradient of elastic region of stress-strain curve = resistance to elastic deformation. A material can be stiff but weak (glass) or flexible but strong (high-strength rubber).

questions.question: Two wires in series: steel (E=200GPa, L=2m, d=2mm) and copper (E=120GPa, L=3m, d=1mm). Force F=500N. Total extension? (π=3.14)
A wire is loaded to the yield point and then unloaded. The stress-strain graph of the unloading follows a straight line parallel to the original elastic region. What can be concluded?
options: The wire has a higher Young's Modulus after loading
The wire has the same Young's Modulus but a permanent plastic strain
The wire has been strengthened and will not yield again
The wire has been permanently compressed
questions.answer: The wire has the same Young's Modulus but a permanent plastic strain
questions.explanation: The unloading line has the same gradient (slope) as the initial elastic region → same E. But it intercepts the strain axis at a non-zero value → permanent plastic strain. The wire has been permanently extended. Work hardening may slightly raise the new yield stress.

questions.question: Which mechanical property is most important for a car suspension spring that must absorb repeated impacts without breaking?
options: High resilience (high elastic energy per unit volume) and good fatigue resistance
High ductility — permanent deformation is acceptable
questions.answer: High resilience (high elastic energy per unit volume) and good fatigue resistance
questions.explanation: A suspension spring must store and release elastic energy repeatedly without fatigue failure. High resilience (large area under elastic part of stress-strain curve) and fatigue resistance are essential. Spring steel (tempered) has these properties.

questions.question: Two springs of spring constants k₁ and k₂ are connected in series. What is the effective spring constant?
questions.explanation: 1/k_series = 1/k₁ + 1/k₂ = (k₁+k₂)/(k₁k₂). So k_series = k₁k₂/(k₁+k₂).

questions.question: In a stress-strain curve for mild steel, the fracture stress is LOWER than the UTS because:
options: The steel softens after the UTS
After UTS, the specimen necks (cross-section decreases) — engineering stress (force/original area) falls even though true stress rises
Temperature drops after UTS causing embrittlement
The testing machine applies less force after UTS
questions.answer: After UTS, the specimen necks (cross-section decreases) — engineering stress (force/original area) falls even though true stress rises
questions.explanation: After UTS, localised necking begins — cross-section at one point decreases. Engineering stress = F/A₀ (original area) falls as F decreases. But true stress (F/actual area) continues rising until fracture. Engineering curve shows fracture below UTS.

questions.question: In a Young's modulus experiment with a wire, doubling the diameter of the wire while keeping all other factors constant would:
options: Reduce the extension to one quarter
Have no effect on the extension
questions.answer: Reduce the extension to one quarter
questions.explanation: ΔL = FL₀/(AE). Area A = πd²/4 ∝ d². If d doubles, A quadruples, so ΔL decreases to ΔL/4.

questions.question: The bungee cord (k=50 N/m, natural length 10m) is attached to a 70 kg person. What is the maximum extension? (g=10)
A glass window and a steel beam have the same cross-section and are subjected to the same tensile force. The steel beam stretches but does not break. The glass cracks. This is because:
options: Glass has a higher Young's Modulus than steel
Glass is brittle — it fractures suddenly without plastic deformation when its fracture stress is reached
Glass has a lower Young's Modulus than steel
questions.answer: Glass is brittle — it fractures suddenly without plastic deformation when its fracture stress is reached
questions.explanation: Glass is brittle — it has essentially no plastic region. When the applied stress reaches its fracture stress, it breaks suddenly with no warning. Steel is ductile — it yields plastically and deforms extensively before fracture. (Glass actually has a similar E to steel.)

questions.question: For a steel specimen at stress 100 MPa (E=200 GPa), the energy stored per unit volume is:
questions.explanation: Energy/volume = ½σε = ½×σ²/E = (10⁸)²/(2×2×10¹¹) = 10¹⁶/(4×10¹¹) = 2.5×10⁴ J/m³ = 25 kJ/m³.
